1: A literary frame: YHWH addresses Moses with the penalty-provisions for the Holiness Code violations — the sanctions that enforce the statutes of chapter 19. The chapter opens with Molech-worship, the worst apostasy (child sacrifice). The escalating severity of the penalties (death, cutting off, childlessness) mirrors the gravity of covenant violation. Christ came not to set aside these moral boundaries but to fulfill the covenant demands himself: 'Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us' (Galatians 3:13). The penalties of Leviticus 20 are the shadow of the curse Christ bore.
2: A shadow: 'Any person — whether Israelite or foreigner... who gives any of his children to Molech shall surely be put to death.' Molech-worship demanded child sacrifice — the darkest of ancient Near Eastern practices, offering a child's life to secure divine favor. The antithesis is the gospel: God does not demand the sacrifice of children — he gives his own Son: 'For God so loved the world, that he gave his only Son' (John 3:16). The Molech-worshipper sacrifices his child to gain life; God sacrifices his Son to give life to others. The law's harshest penalty guards the boundary that the gospel crosses from the opposite side.
3: A shadow: 'I will set my face against that man and will cut him off from among his people, because he has given one of his children to Molech, so as to make my sanctuary unclean and to profane my holy name.' The divine judgment ('I will set my face against') is the language of covenant wrath. Christ, bearing the covenant curse at the cross, faces the precise opposite: God does not set his face against Christ in permanent rejection but vindicates him in the resurrection. 'My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?' (Matthew 27:46) is the temporary moment of forsakenness that secures the permanent 'I will never leave you nor forsake you' (Hebrews 13:5) for those united to Christ.
4: A shadow: 'If the people of the land close their eyes to what that man does when he gives his children to Molech, and do not put him to death, then I will set my face against that man and his family.' The community bears responsibility for enforcing covenant standards — silence in the face of apostasy is complicity. Christ confronts this in his prophetic ministry: 'Woe to you, scribes and Pharisees, hypocrites! For you shut the kingdom of heaven in people's faces' (Matthew 23:13). The church is called not to 'close its eyes' to covenant violation but to speak the truth in love (Ephesians 4:15), as Christ did without flinching.
5: A shadow: 'I will set my face against that man and his family and will cut off from among their people him and all who follow him in whoring after Molech.' The judgment extends to those who follow apostasy, not merely the instigator. Paul warns: 'Do not be deceived: bad company ruins good morals' (1 Corinthians 15:33). Christ separates the gathered from the scattered: 'Whoever is not with me is against me, and whoever does not gather with me scatters' (Matthew 12:30). The cutting-off judgment points to the final separation Christ describes: 'I never knew you; depart from me, you workers of lawlessness' (Matthew 7:23).
6: A shadow: 'If a person turns to mediums and necromancers, whoring after them, I will set my face against that person and will cut him off from among his people.' The consulting of the dead for guidance is the spiritual adultery that the law calls 'whoring' (zinnah). Christ is the living Lord who renders all necromancy obsolete: 'I am the first and the last, and the living one. I died, and behold I am alive forevermore, and I have the keys of Death and Hades' (Revelation 1:17-18). The one who holds the keys of death is the only legitimate guide through death — seeking the dead for guidance is unnecessary when the risen Christ speaks by his Spirit.
7: A direct quotation type: 'Consecrate yourselves, therefore, and be holy, for I am YHWH your God.' Peter quotes this verse alongside Leviticus 19:2 in 1 Peter 1:15-16. The consecration-call is fulfilled in Christ's high-priestly prayer: 'Sanctify them in the truth; your word is truth. As you sent me into the world, so I have sent them into the world. And for their sake I consecrate myself, that they also may be sanctified in truth' (John 17:17-19). Christ consecrates himself so that his people may be consecrated — the source of all holiness is not human effort but union with the Holy One who has set himself apart for our sake.
8: A direct revelation: 'Keep my statutes and do them; I am YHWH who sanctifies you.' The divine self-identification as the one who sanctifies — YHWH mekadishchem, 'YHWH your sanctifier' — is one of the most important covenant titles in Leviticus. Paul applies the sanctification title to Christ: 'Jesus Christ, who became to us wisdom from God, righteousness and sanctification and redemption' (1 Corinthians 1:30). Christ is YHWH-who-sanctifies in person — the eternal 'I am YHWH' has a name: Jesus, who sanctifies 'those who draw near to God through him' (Hebrews 7:25).
9: A shadow: 'For anyone who curses his father or his mother shall surely be put to death.' The fifth commandment carries a death penalty for its violation at the extreme — the covenantal severity mirrors the seriousness of the parent-honor obligation. Christ himself bore 'the curse of the law' (Galatians 3:13) in his death — not for his own violation (he fulfilled all the commandments) but for ours. The severity of Leviticus 20:9 is the legal backdrop for the NT's theology of Christ as the substitute who bore the death-penalty on behalf of covenant-breakers. 'Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us — for it is written, Cursed is everyone who is hanged on a tree' (Galatians 3:13).
10: A shadow: 'If a man commits adultery with the wife of his neighbor, both the adulterer and the adulteress shall surely be put to death.' When the scribes and Pharisees brought a woman caught in adultery to Jesus (John 8:3-11), they invoked Leviticus 20:10 — the law requiring death. Jesus's response — 'Let him who is without sin among you be the first to throw a stone' (John 8:7) — does not abolish the law but exposes that no accuser can stand before it either. He then forgives: 'Neither do I condemn you; go, and from now on sin no more.' Christ fulfills the law's demand by bearing it himself, and transforms its enforcement from condemnation to the call to repentance.
11: A shadow: incest (father's wife) carries the death penalty — 'their blood is upon them.' The escalating sexual holiness laws define the purity of the covenant community. Paul confronts a case in Corinth (1 Corinthians 5:1-5) — a man living with his father's wife — and quotes Leviticus 20:11 implicitly by demanding severe discipline: 'hand this man over to Satan for the destruction of the flesh, so that his spirit may be saved in the day of the Lord.' The church, as the new Israel, maintains the covenant purity boundaries, but now the goal is restoration, not merely punishment — the discipline serves the ultimate hope of redemption.
12: A shadow: 'If a man lies with his daughter-in-law, both of them shall surely be put to death.' The death penalties for incest establish the absolute boundaries of the family covenant-structure. Christ expands the family covenant while maintaining its purity: 'Whoever does the will of God, he is my brother and sister and mother' (Mark 3:35). The kingdom family is constituted by covenant fidelity to God, not natural descent — and it maintains, in the Spirit, the same purity that the Levitical law mandated externally.
13: A shadow: the death penalty for male homosexual intercourse is one of the Levitical boundaries Paul invokes in Romans 1:24-27 as evidence of the suppression of created order. Paul's point in Romans 1 is that the escalating moral disorder described there — including the abandonment of natural relations — is God's judgment (giving them over) upon a world that refuses to honor God. Christ's redemption reverses this disorder: 'such were some of you. But you were washed, you were sanctified, you were justified in the name of the Lord Jesus Christ and by the Spirit of our God' (1 Corinthians 6:11). The law names the disorder; the gospel names the transformation.
14: A shadow: the prohibition of taking both a woman and her mother, with a death by fire. The sexual ethics of Leviticus 20 protect the integrity of the family unit — the basic building block of the covenant community. Christ presents himself as the one who ultimately unifies the family in a deeper sense: 'everyone who has left houses or brothers or sisters or father or mother or children or lands, for my name's sake, will receive a hundredfold and will inherit eternal life' (Matthew 19:29). The kingdom family supersedes the biological family without abolishing it.
15: A shadow: bestiality — death for both man and animal. The death penalties in Leviticus 20 together constitute the covenant community's absolute boundary — the 'not this' by which the holy community is defined. Christ defines his people positively: 'my sheep hear my voice' (John 10:27). The laws of Leviticus 20 are the negative boundary-markers; the gospel is the positive call that brings people into the covenant community — called, washed, and renewed to reflect YHWH's holiness in positive obedience.
16: A shadow: the same death penalty applies to the woman in the case of Leviticus 20:16. The equal culpability of both parties reflects YHWH's impartial justice — no double standard. Christ's impartiality in judgment: 'God shows no partiality' (Romans 2:11). Paul applies this to the gospel: 'there is neither male nor female, for you are all one in Christ Jesus' (Galatians 3:28). The covenant boundaries apply equally; the covenant grace is also equally available — 'there is no distinction: for all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God, and are justified by his grace as a gift' (Romans 3:22-24).
17: A shadow: incest with a sister — 'a disgrace, and they shall be cut off in the sight of the children of their people.' The concept of 'cutting off' (karet) from the people is the covenant's internal exile — the offender is separated from YHWH's community and its blessings. Christ reverses karet by suffering the ultimate cutting off — 'he was cut off from the land of the living, stricken for the transgression of my people' (Isaiah 53:8). The exile of the covenant-breaker is borne by the covenant-keeper, so that those who deserve cutting off may be grafted in (Romans 11:17-24).
18: A shadow: the prohibition of intercourse during menstruation. The purity laws create a structure of separation and reunion — the monthly cycle of distance and return mirrors the broader OT pattern of exile and restoration, judgment and reconciliation. Christ abolishes the separation: the woman with the flow of blood who touched the hem of his garment (Mark 5:25-34) — ritually unclean, excluded from full temple access — is healed instantly, and Jesus calls her 'daughter.' The one who made her unclean touches him and makes him, as it were, responsible for her condition; instead, his holiness flows out and cleanses her.
19: A shadow: the prohibitions against intercourse with an aunt. The increasingly specific incest prohibitions in Leviticus 20 enumerate the covenant family's integrity. These laws gave Israel's family structure its distinctive covenant purity, setting it apart from surrounding cultures. The church inherits a transformed version of this family purity — not biological relations but spiritual ones. The community that is 'built up as a spiritual house' (1 Peter 2:5) maintains its integrity through Christ, 'the cornerstone' (1 Peter 2:6) who defines the shape of the covenant household.
20: A shadow: 'If a man lies with his uncle's wife, he has uncovered his uncle's nakedness; they shall bear their sin; they shall die childless.' The consequence of 'dying childless' resonates with the Abrahamic covenant: the promise was carried through offspring, and childlessness was the covenant curse. Christ reverses the spiritual childlessness of sin: 'I will give him... an everlasting name that shall not be cut off' (Isaiah 56:5). Through Christ, those who were spiritually cut off from the covenant become children of God: 'to all who received him, who believed in his name, he gave the right to become children of God' (John 1:12).
21: A shadow: 'If a man takes his brother's wife, it is impurity.' John the Baptist rebuked Herod for exactly this violation — 'It is not lawful for you to have your brother's wife' (Mark 6:18). His fidelity to Leviticus 20:21 cost him his head (Mark 6:27). The Baptist's martyrdom for covenant-truth is the type of Christ's own death: both are killed by political-religious collusion for speaking uncomfortable truth. 'A servant is not greater than his master. If they persecuted me, they will also persecute you' (John 15:20). The prophet who dies for covenant fidelity anticipates the Prophet who dies for covenant fulfillment.
22: A shadow: 'You shall therefore keep all my statutes and all my rules and do them, that the land where I am bringing you to live may not vomit you out.' The land of Canaan is the type of the kingdom of God — and the kingdom can expel the unfaithful. Christ teaches this with the parable of the wedding garment (Matthew 22:11-13): entry is by grace, but complacent covenant-violation leads to exclusion. 'Every branch in me that does not bear fruit he takes away' (John 15:2). The promised land's threat of vomiting up the disobedient points to the kingdom's eschatological seriousness.
23: A shadow: 'And you shall not walk in the customs of the nation that I am driving out before you, for they did all these things, and therefore I detested them.' The ethical distinctiveness of Israel from the surrounding nations is grounded in YHWH's own standard — not cultural preference but divine character. Christ calls his people to the same counter-cultural distinctiveness: 'You are the light of the world' (Matthew 5:14); 'Do not be conformed to this world' (Romans 12:2). The holiness-distance from surrounding culture that Leviticus legislated is now the Spirit-formed character of the new covenant community.
24: A shadow: 'I am YHWH your God, who has separated you from the peoples... to be mine.' The election-separation formula — 'I have set you apart' — is the covenant's foundational claim on Israel. Paul applies it to the church: 'But you are a chosen race, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, a people for his own possession' (1 Peter 2:9, quoting Exodus 19:6; also Deuteronomy 7:6). Christ is the one through whom this election-separation reaches the Gentiles — 'you once were not a people, but now you are God's people' (1 Peter 2:10). The Lord who separated Israel from the nations has in Christ separated a new covenant people from every nation.
25: A shadow: 'You shall therefore separate the clean beast from the unclean, and the unclean bird from the clean.' The clean/unclean animal distinction, established in Lev 11, is here applied in the broader context of Israel's separation from the nations. Peter's vision in Acts 10:9-16 — 'What God has made clean, do not call common' — directly abrogates this distinction. The clean/unclean food law was the physical enactment of the Jew/Gentile distinction; when Christ declares 'all foods clean' (Mark 7:19), the ethnic-boundary significance of the law is abolished in principle. The Gentile mission flows directly from this fulfillment.
26: A direct revelation: 'You shall be holy to me, for I YHWH am holy and have separated you from the peoples, that you should be mine.' This is the fullest statement of the Holiness Code's purpose: holiness is not a legal achievement but a relational status — set apart to belong to YHWH. In Christ, the belonging is made explicit in its fullest form: 'you are not your own, for you were bought with a price' (1 Corinthians 6:19-20). The church belongs to Christ as Israel belonged to YHWH — called out (ekklesia), purchased, and set apart. The 'you should be mine' of Leviticus 20:26 echoes in the covenant formula of Revelation: 'They will be his people, and God himself will be with them as their God' (Revelation 21:3).
27: A shadow: 'A man or a woman who is a medium or a necromancer shall surely be put to death.' The death penalty for false spiritual mediation closes the chapter — the law protects the exclusive channel of divine communication. Christ as the sole mediator makes all other mediation unnecessary and therefore illegitimate: 'there is one mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus, who gave himself as a ransom for all' (1 Timothy 2:5-6). The finality of the death penalty for false mediation in Leviticus 20:27 corresponds to the NT's insistence that 'there is salvation in no one else, for there is no other name under heaven given among men by which we must be saved' (Acts 4:12).
